description: Multi-paragraph long-document corpus for slider drift bench scale verification (Phase E.1 v0.6). Each doc is 200-400 words across 2-4 paragraphs, expected to yield 15-30 source triples post-extraction. Validates whether the v0.4-verified preservation claim (median 1.000, p10 0.818 on 4-12 triple docs) generalises to longer documents. Hand-authored from common factual knowledge to avoid copyright entanglement.

documents.text: The Internet originated as ARPANET, a project of the United States Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency in the late 1960s. The first message was transmitted between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute in October 1969. ARPANET initially connected four nodes at universities in California and Utah. Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn developed the TCP/IP protocol suite during the 1970s, which became the foundation for modern internet communication. The protocols were standardised in 1983 when ARPANET officially transitioned to TCP/IP. Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989, introducing HTML, HTTP, and the first web browser. The Mosaic browser was released by NCSA in 1993, making the web accessible to non-technical users. The Internet became a global commercial network during the mid-1990s following the deprecation of restrictions on commercial traffic. ICANN was established in 1998 to coordinate the allocation of domain names and IP addresses.

Quantum mechanics emerged in the early twentieth century as physicists confronted phenomena that classical mechanics could not explain. Max Planck introduced the concept of quantised energy in 1900 to resolve the ultraviolet catastrophe in blackbody radiation. Albert Einstein extended this idea in 1905 by proposing that light itself is quantised into photons, explaining the photoelectric effect. Niels Bohr formulated his model of the hydrogen atom in 1913, postulating discrete electron orbits. Louis de Broglie hypothesised wave-particle duality in 1924, suggesting all matter exhibits wave-like properties. Werner Heisenberg developed matrix mechanics in 1925 and articulated the uncertainty principle in 1927. Erwin Schrödinger formulated his wave equation in 1926, providing an alternative formalism that proved equivalent to matrix mechanics. The Copenhagen interpretation, articulated primarily by Bohr and Heisenberg, became the dominant philosophical framework for understanding quantum measurement. Paul Dirac unified quantum mechanics with special relativity in 1928, predicting the existence of antimatter, which Carl Anderson observed experimentally in 1932.

Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, presenting natural selection as the mechanism for evolutionary change. Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at similar conclusions, prompting their joint presentation to the Linnean Society in 1858. Gregor Mendel published his foundational work on inheritance in 1866, establishing the basic principles of genetics through his experiments on pea plants. The synthesis of Darwinian evolution with Mendelian genetics formed the modern evolutionary synthesis during the 1930s and 1940s. James Watson and Francis Crick determined the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953, building on the X-ray diffraction work of Rosalind Franklin. The discovery of DNA enabled molecular evolution to become a quantitative discipline. Kimura proposed the neutral theory of molecular evolution in 1968, arguing that most genetic variation results from random drift rather than selection. The Human Genome Project completed its sequencing of the human genome in 2003, after thirteen years of international collaboration.

Rome was founded according to legend in 753 BCE by Romulus, who became its first king. The Roman Republic was established in 509 BCE following the overthrow of the last Etruscan king, Tarquinius Superbus. The Republic governed through elected consuls and the Senate, expanding gradually across the Italian peninsula. The Punic Wars against Carthage occurred between 264 and 146 BCE, ending with the destruction of Carthage and Roman dominance in the western Mediterranean. Julius Caesar was assassinated in the Senate on the Ides of March in 44 BCE, ending his dictatorship. Caesar's adopted heir Octavian defeated Mark Antony at the Battle of Actium in 31 BCE, becoming the first Roman emperor under the title Augustus. The Roman Empire reached its territorial peak under Trajan in 117 CE. Constantine the Great legalised Christianity through the Edict of Milan in 313 CE. The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 CE when Odoacer deposed the last emperor, Romulus Augustulus, while the Eastern Roman Empire continued as the Byzantine Empire until 1453.

The Solar System formed approximately 4.6 billion years ago from a collapsing cloud of gas and dust. The Sun contains approximately 99.86 percent of the system's mass and powers itself through nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium. Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and the smallest in the Solar System. Venus has the densest atmosphere of any terrestrial planet and the highest surface temperature, exceeding 460 degrees Celsius. Earth is the only known planet to harbour life and possesses one natural satellite, the Moon. Mars has two small moons, Phobos and Deimos, and shows extensive evidence of past liquid water. The asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter contains millions of rocky bodies, the largest being Ceres. Jupiter is the largest planet, more massive than all other planets combined, and has at least 95 known moons. Saturn is famous for its prominent ring system composed primarily of ice particles. Uranus rotates on its side with an axial tilt of 98 degrees. Neptune is the windiest planet, with winds reaching 2,100 kilometres per hour. Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006 by the International Astronomical Union.

Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts proposed the first mathematical model of artificial neurons in 1943. Frank Rosenblatt invented the perceptron in 1958, the first trainable neural network capable of learning simple patterns. The perceptron's limitations were exposed by Minsky and Papert in their 1969 book Perceptrons, contributing to the first AI winter. The backpropagation algorithm was popularised by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in 1986, enabling efficient training of multi-layer networks. Yann LeCun applied convolutional neural networks to handwritten digit recognition in 1989, demonstrating their power for visual tasks. Long short-term memory networks were introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997 to address the vanishing gradient problem in recurrent networks. AlexNet won the ImageNet competition in 2012 by a large margin, marking the beginning of the deep learning era. The Transformer architecture was introduced by Vaswani and colleagues in 2017, replacing recurrent layers with self-attention mechanisms. GPT-3 was released by OpenAI in 2020 with 175 billion parameters, demonstrating emergent few-shot learning capabilities.

Svante Arrhenius first calculated in 1896 that doubling atmospheric carbon dioxide could raise global temperatures. Charles Keeling began continuous measurements of atmospheric CO2 at Mauna Loa Observatory in 1958, producing the iconic Keeling Curve that documents the steady rise in concentration. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change was established in 1988 by the United Nations Environment Programme and the World Meteorological Organization. The IPCC publishes comprehensive assessment reports synthesising climate science from thousands of researchers. The Kyoto Protocol was adopted in 1997, establishing binding emissions reduction targets for developed nations. The Paris Agreement was signed in 2015, with nearly all nations committing to limit warming below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels. Global mean surface temperature has risen approximately 1.2 degrees Celsius since the late nineteenth century. Arctic sea ice extent has declined approximately 13 percent per decade since satellite observations began in 1979. Atmospheric CO2 concentration exceeded 420 parts per million in 2024, the highest in at least 800,000 years.

Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1905, abolishing the concept of absolute simultaneity and establishing the constancy of the speed of light in vacuum. Special relativity predicts that moving clocks run slower and that mass and energy are equivalent through the equation E equals mc squared. Einstein extended his framework to general relativity in 1915, describing gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy. General relativity predicted that light from distant stars would be deflected by the Sun's gravity, which Arthur Eddington confirmed during the solar eclipse of 1919. The theory predicted gravitational redshift, gravitational time dilation, and the expansion of the universe. Karl Schwarzschild derived the first exact solution to Einstein's field equations in 1916, describing what later became known as black holes. Edwin Hubble observed in 1929 that distant galaxies are receding from Earth at speeds proportional to their distance, supporting the expansion prediction. The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory directly detected gravitational waves from merging black holes in September 2015, confirming a century-old prediction.

The French Revolution began in 1789 amid economic crisis, political grievances, and Enlightenment ideals about liberty and equality. The Estates-General convened at Versailles in May 1789 for the first time since 1614. The Third Estate declared itself the National Assembly on 17 June 1789 and pledged the Tennis Court Oath three days later. The Bastille fortress was stormed by Parisians on 14 July 1789, becoming the symbolic beginning of the Revolution. The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen was adopted in August 1789, articulating universal principles of liberty and legal equality. King Louis XVI attempted to flee France in June 1791 but was captured at Varennes. Louis XVI was tried for treason and executed by guillotine in January 1793. Maximilien Robespierre led the Reign of Terror from 1793 to 1794, during which thousands were executed. Robespierre himself was executed in July 1794, ending the Terror. Napoleon Bonaparte seized power through the coup of 18 Brumaire in November 1799 and was crowned Emperor of the French in 1804.

The cell theory was formulated by Matthias Schleiden and Theodor Schwann in 1838 and 1839, asserting that all living organisms consist of cells. Robert Hooke first observed cells in cork tissue and coined the term in 1665. Anton van Leeuwenhoek observed living single-celled organisms in 1674 using microscopes of his own design. Rudolf Virchow added the principle in 1855 that all cells arise from pre-existing cells. Eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles including the nucleus, mitochondria, and endoplasmic reticulum. Prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Mitochondria contain their own circular DNA and are believed to have originated through endosymbiosis of ancient bacteria. Lynn Margulis developed the endosymbiotic theory in detail during the 1960s and 1970s. The mitochondrial genome was first sequenced for humans in 1981 by Anderson and colleagues. Plant cells contain chloroplasts, which conduct photosynthesis and similarly originated through endosymbiosis from cyanobacteria-like ancestors.

The Second World War began on 1 September 1939 when Germany invaded Poland under Adolf Hitler's leadership. The United Kingdom and France declared war on Germany two days later, honouring their guarantees to Poland. Germany conquered France in June 1940 after a six-week campaign. The Battle of Britain in summer and autumn of 1940 was the first major battle fought entirely in the air. Germany invaded the Soviet Union in June 1941 in Operation Barbarossa, opening the largest military front in history. Japan attacked the United States naval base at Pearl Harbor on 7 December 1941, bringing the United States into the war. The Battle of Stalingrad ended in February 1943 with a decisive Soviet victory. The Allied invasion of Normandy began on 6 June 1944, opening the Western European front. Germany surrendered unconditionally on 8 May 1945 following the fall of Berlin. The United States dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945. Japan formally surrendered on 2 September 1945 aboard the USS Missouri.

Dmitri Mendeleev published the first widely accepted periodic table of the elements in 1869, organising elements by atomic weight and chemical properties. Mendeleev left gaps in his table for elements not yet discovered and predicted their properties. Gallium was discovered in 1875, germanium in 1886, and scandium in 1879, each closely matching Mendeleev's predictions. Henry Moseley reorganised the periodic table by atomic number rather than atomic weight in 1913, resolving inconsistencies in Mendeleev's arrangement. The discovery of isotopes by Frederick Soddy in 1913 explained why atomic weights were not always integer values. Glenn Seaborg discovered plutonium in 1940 and several transuranic elements thereafter, contributing to the actinide series organisation. The modern periodic table contains 118 confirmed elements as of 2024. Elements 95 through 118 are all synthetic and were created in particle accelerators or nuclear reactors. The IUPAC officially named oganesson element 118 in 2016.

The Human Genome Project was launched in 1990 as an international collaborative effort to sequence the entire human genome. The project was coordinated primarily by the United States National Institutes of Health and the Department of Energy. Celera Genomics, a private company led by Craig Venter, began parallel sequencing efforts in 1998 using the shotgun sequencing method. Both efforts published initial drafts of the human genome simultaneously in February 2001. The complete genome sequence was declared finished in April 2003, two years ahead of schedule. The human genome contains approximately 3 billion base pairs distributed across 23 chromosome pairs. Initial estimates suggested 100,000 genes; current estimates place the count at approximately 20,000 protein-coding genes. The Encyclopedia of DNA Elements project followed the Human Genome Project to characterise functional elements in the genome. Whole genome sequencing costs have fallen from approximately 3 billion dollars for the original project to under 1,000 dollars by 2014.

Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman published the first paper on public-key cryptography in 1976, proposing key exchange without prior shared secrets. Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman developed the RSA cryptosystem in 1977, providing the first practical public-key encryption and signature scheme. RSA security depends on the difficulty of factoring large composite numbers. Neal Koblitz and Victor Miller independently proposed elliptic curve cryptography in 1985, offering equivalent security with shorter keys. The Advanced Encryption Standard was selected in 2001 from the Rijndael algorithm by Vincent Rijmen and Joan Daemen. AES became the United States federal standard for symmetric encryption, replacing the older Data Encryption Standard. Phil Zimmermann released Pretty Good Privacy in 1991, bringing public-key cryptography to ordinary computer users. The SHA-256 hash function was published by the National Security Agency in 2001 as part of the SHA-2 family. Quantum computers threaten current public-key systems through Shor's algorithm, motivating ongoing research in post-quantum cryptography.

The Industrial Revolution began in Britain during the late eighteenth century and transformed economies across Europe and North America. James Watt patented his improved steam engine in 1769, drastically increasing efficiency over earlier designs. The textile industry was mechanised through inventions including the spinning jenny by James Hargreaves in 1764 and the water frame by Richard Arkwright in 1769. Edmund Cartwright invented the power loom in 1785, enabling automated weaving. The first factory system emerged in textile production, replacing decentralised cottage industries. Iron production was revolutionised by Abraham Darby's use of coke instead of charcoal in 1709 and by Henry Bessemer's converter process patented in 1856. The Stockton and Darlington Railway opened in 1825 as the first public railway to use steam locomotives. The Liverpool and Manchester Railway began service in 1830, demonstrating the commercial viability of railways. Urbanisation accelerated dramatically as workers migrated from rural areas to industrial cities, reshaping social structures across the affected nations.

The immune system protects organisms from pathogens through a coordinated network of cells, tissues, and signalling molecules. Innate immunity provides immediate non-specific defence through physical barriers, phagocytic cells, and inflammation. Adaptive immunity develops over time and produces highly specific responses against particular pathogens. T cells mature in the thymus and recognise antigens presented by major histocompatibility complex molecules. B cells mature in the bone marrow and produce antibodies against extracellular pathogens. Edward Jenner pioneered vaccination in 1796 by demonstrating that cowpox exposure protected against smallpox. Louis Pasteur developed attenuated vaccines for rabies and anthrax in the 1880s, establishing the scientific basis of immunisation. Frank Macfarlane Burnet proposed the clonal selection theory in 1957, explaining antibody specificity. Susumu Tonegawa elucidated the genetic mechanism of antibody diversity in 1976, earning the Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine. The first mRNA vaccines for COVID-19 received emergency authorisation in December 2020 from the United States and the United Kingdom.
